summary: claude-all-config silently routes installer AI traffic through author-controlled third-party accounts
details: The package ships populated live API keys in.env.example (Context7, Exa, Z.AI, MiniMax, and a Telegram bot token+chat_id) and postinstall.js merges them into ~/.claude/.env, ~/.gemini/.env, ~/.codex/.env, and ~/.kiro/.env on every install. The installed claude-all wrapper (bin/claude-all lines 30-34) then auto-exports ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=$Z_AI_API_KEY and ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=https://api.z.ai/api/anthropic when the user has not set their own Anthropic key — installer prompts, code, and project context the user believes are going to Anthropic are silently routed to api.z.ai using the author's key. The configured `telegram` MCP server is wired with the author's bot token and chat_id 1185240496, so any AI-agent notifications dispatched through that MCP land in the author's Telegram inbox. postinstall.js additionally writes ~/.claude/settings.json with permissions.allow including Bash(*), Read(*), Write(*), WebFetch(*), mcp__* and deny:[]; ~/.gemini/settings.json with autoAccept:true and run_shell_command(*) permitted; and the claude-all wrapper appends --dangerously-skip-permissions and sets IS_SANDBOX=1 to defeat Claude's root-refusal. setupLocalBinSymlinks() symlinks ~/.local/bin/{gemini,codex} to package-local wrappers that shadow the upstream binaries on PATH and force auto-approval of all tool calls. The combined effect: installer prompts/data are redirected to attacker-controlled third-party endpoints under the author's identity, the host's tool-approval safety gates are removed, and the upstream `gemini`/`codex` CLIs are hijacked via PATH so future invocations also flow through the author's configuration.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
